content:
3 min read
Just now

--

A beginner’s perspective after my first year of learning programming.
Press enter or click to view image in full size

When I first started learning programming, I believed that if I watched enough tutorials and completed my syllabus, I would become a good developer.
I was excited to learn HTML, CSS, JavaScript, React, SQL, Python, and backend development. Every new technology looked interesting, and I wanted to learn everything as quickly as possible.
But after spending months learning and practicing, I realized that programming is very different from what I imagined.
Here are seven lessons that nobody told me when I started.
1. Tutorials Feel Easy. Building Alone Feels Impossible.
Watching someone code for an hour and understanding every line feels great. It tricks you into thinking you know the topic.
Then you close the video, open a blank file, and freeze.
Tutorials give you the illusion of learning because you’re following someone else’s thinking. Real learning starts the moment you try to build something on your own, without anyone guiding your hands.
2. Errors Are Not the Enemy. They’re the Actual Teacher.
In the beginning, every red line of error text felt like proof that I wasn’t cut out for this.
Now I understand errors are just the computer telling you exactly what’s wrong, and half the job of being a developer is learning how to read that message instead of panicking at it.
The developers who look “naturally good” at coding aren’t people who don’t get errors. They’re people who got comfortable Googling them.
3. You Will Forget Things. That’s Normal, Not a Failure.
I used to think that if I forgot a concept I had already learned, it meant I wasn’t smart enough for this field.
The truth is, you don’t need to memorize every syntax or function. You need to remember that it exists and know where to look it up. Professional developers Google basic things all the time. What matters is understanding the logic, not memorizing every detail.
4. Comparing Yourself to Others Online Will Slow You Down
Scrolling through LinkedIn or Twitter and seeing people your age building full apps, landing internships, or talking about advanced tools can be discouraging.
But everyone’s starting point and speed is different. Some people started earlier, some have more time, some have guidance you don’t have. Comparing your Chapter 1 to someone else’s Chapter 10 only kills your motivation for no real reason.
5. Consistency Beats Intensity, Every Single Time
I used to code for six hours straight on a Sunday and then not touch my laptop for the rest of the week.
That pattern doesn’t build skill. What actually helped me improve was showing up for even 30–45 minutes daily, even on days I didn’t feel like it. Small, repeated effort compounds. Bursts of motivation don’t.
6. Understanding “Why” Matters More Than Copying “How”
In the beginning, I used to copy code from tutorials or the internet just to get things working, without understanding why it worked.
It felt productive, but it wasn’t. The moment something broke, I had no idea how to fix it because I never understood the logic in the first place. Now I try to slow down and ask “why does this work” before moving to the next line.
7. You Don’t Need to Know Everything to Start Building
I used to think I needed to master HTML, CSS, JavaScript, backend, and databases before I could build anything real.
That belief kept me stuck in “learning mode” for way too long. The truth is, you can start building small projects with the basics you already know. You learn the rest along the way, project by project, not by waiting until you feel “ready enough.”
-
I’m still learning.
I still make mistakes almost every day.
But I’ve realized that becoming a developer isn’t about knowing everything.
It’s about staying curious, practicing consistently, and learning one step at a time.
This is my first Medium article, and I hope it encourages another beginner who is just starting their journey.
Thank you for reading.

I’m Mrunal Kalsait, currently in my first year of learning programming, exploring web development, Python, backend development, and AI. This is part of an ongoing series documenting my journey — the confusion, the small wins, and everything in between — for anyone else who’s just getting started.
